The normal swallow is a complex and dynamic physiologic  
process involving coordination between multiple sensorimotor 
systems to move food or liquid bolus through the mouth, throat, 
and esophagus into the stomach while protecting the airway. 
Neurologic control of swallowing involves an intricate neuronal 
network with both voluntary and reflexive components, which 
regulate more than 30 paired muscles with sensory and motor 
information traveling over six cranial nerves. 97 Swallowing im -
pairment, known as dysphagia, can result from a wide variety of 
functional or structural deficits of the oral cavity, pharynx, larynx, 
or esophagus and can occur at any time across the lifespan. This 
chapter provides a broad overview of swallowing, including the 
physiology and pathophysiology of eating and swallowing, as well 
as assessment and treatment methods.
Swallowing Physiology
Swallowing comprises reflexive and voluntary movement pat -
terns that change as individuals develop, but the overall sequenc -
ing of biomechanical events remains stable over the lifespan. The 
act of swallowing has been described using the four-stage sequen-
tial model for drinking and the process model for eating.  
The four-stage model (Fig. 3.1A), which includes the oral prepa -
ratory, oral propulsive, pharyngeal, and esophageal stages,  
adequately describes the biomechanics and bolus movement of  
liquids; however, this model cannot represent the bolus move -
ment and process of eating food. T o describe the mechanism of 
eating and swallowing solid food, the process model of feeding 
was developed (see Fig. 3.1B).33,86
Oral Phase—Drinking
Preparatory Stage
The oral preparatory stage of swallowing is a voluntary phase that 
occurs within the oral cavity. The bolus is taken into the mouth 
and held in the anterior portion of the floor of the mouth on the 
tongue surface against the hard palate. Simultaneously, the lips 
maintain a seal to prevent anterior spillage. The oral cavity seals 
posteriorly by contact of the soft palate and tongue to prevent 
the bolus from leaking into the oropharynx before swallowing 
initiation.70
Propulsive Stage
The goal of the oral propulsive stage is to successfully transport 
the bolus from the oral cavity to the pharynx. During this volun -
tary phase of swallowing, the tongue tip elevates and contacts the 
alveolar ridge of the hard palate just behind the upper incisors.  
In contrast, the posterior portion of the tongue depresses to open 
the back of the oral cavity. As the tongue surface elevates, the area 
of tongue-palate contact expands from anterior to posterior, pro -
pelling the bolus into the pharynx. The pharyngeal phase of the 
swallow normally begins during oral propulsion when drinking.70
Oral Phase—Eating (Process Model of Feeding)
The four-stage model has limited utility for describing the process 
of normal eating in humans because of the overlap among the oral 
preparatory, oral propulsive, and pharyngeal stages.25,33,86 Therefore 
the process model of feeding, originating in studies of mammalian 
instrumental assessment of swallowing to correctly identify  
the specific biomechanical components of the individual’s 
swallow function to target for compensatory or restorative  
interventions.
• The goal of treatment for dysphagia is to restore swallowing 
skills and maximize quality of life while continuously main -
taining adequate nutrition, hydration, means of medication 
administration, and safe (aspiration-free) swallowing for the 
individual.
3
Rehabilitation of Swallowing Disorders
MICHELE L. SINGER, KEIKO AIHARA, AND MARLÍS GONZALEZ-FERNANDEZ
• Swallowing is a rapid, yet complex sequence of both voluntary 
and reflexive movements comprised of multiple phases.
• Cortical and subcortical regions of the brain play an integral 
part in mediating swallow function, especially the voluntary 
phases of the swallow (oral preparatory and oral propulsive).
• Dysphagia can occur because of a wide variety of functional or 
structural deficits and sensory or motor impairment and can 
lead to serious complications, including dehydration, malnutri-
tion, pneumonia, or airway obstruction.
• A patient-centered, multidisciplinary approach to assessment 
and therapeutic intervention for dysphagia often includes an

humans eat solid food, triturated (chewed and moistened) food 
commonly passes through the faucial arches for bolus formation in 
the oropharynx and vallecula several seconds before pharyngeal 
stage swallow initiation. Additional portions of food may also pass 
into the oropharynx and accumulate there while food remains in 
the oral cavity, and chewing continues.
70
Stage I Transport
Once food has been taken into the mouth, the tongue carries  
the solid food to the postcanine region and then rotates laterally, 
placing food on the occlusal surface of the lower teeth for food 
processing.
70
Food Processing
Immediately following stage I transport, food processing occurs. 
During this process, food is masticated and softened by salivation 
until the consistency is optimal for swallowing. Throughout this 
rhythmic process, the tongue moves mediolaterally and rotates on 
its long axis to allow food to remain on the occlusal surfaces of the 
teeth. The cyclic movement of the jaw varies among food consis -
tencies and is tightly coordinated with the movements of the 
tongue, cheek, soft palate, and hyoid bone, which permits open 
communication between the oral cavity and pharynx. Because 
there is no sealing of the posterior oral cavity during eating, the

ments pump air into the nasal cavity through the pharynx, deliv-
ering aroma to chemoreceptors in the nose. 5,36,85
Stage II Transport
Once processed, food is placed on the tongue’s surface and pro -
pelled back through the faucial pillars to the oropharynx. The 
tongue primarily drives this action without the need for gravity. 
Stage II transport of solids is similar to the oral propulsive stage for 
liquids. However, prepared foods often accumulate on the tongue’s 
pharyngeal surface and vallecula while chewing continues in the 
oral cavity. The aggregated bolus in the oropharynx continues  
to enlarge by subsequent stage II transport cycles, lasting from a 
fraction of a second to 10 seconds in normal individuals before the 
initiation of the pharyngeal stage of the swallow.
70
Pharyngeal Stage
As the bolus is propelled past the anterior faucial pillars, mecha -
noreceptors of the tongue, epiglottis, and pyriform fossa are 
activated, which trigger the pharyngeal phase of the swallow.
40 
The pharyngeal stage of the swallow is a rapid sequence of  
events that occurs within 1 second. This stage of the swallow has 
two essential goals: (1) passage of food through the pharynx  
and upper esophageal sphincter (UES) to the esophagus and

(2) preventing airway compromise (i.e., protecting the larynx 
and trachea during food transport to prevent the food from 
entering the airway). 70
During the pharyngeal stage, the soft palate elevates and  
retracts as the pharyngeal wall compresses along the soft palate, 
closing the nasopharynx and preventing bolus regurgitation into 
the nasal cavity. As the bolus passes into the pharynx, the base of 
the tongue retracts posteriorly, pushing the bolus against the pha-
ryngeal wall. The pharynx shortens vertically to reduce the vol -
ume of the pharyngeal cavity while the pharyngeal constrictors 
contract sequentially from top to bottom, propelling the bolus 
downward toward the esophagus.70
As the bolus passes through the pharynx, the true vocal folds 
adduct to seal the glottis (the space between the vocal folds), 
the false vocal folds approximate, and the arytenoids tilt for -
ward while the epiglottis inverts for arytenoid-epiglottic con -
tact before opening of the UES. 81,94 The hyolaryngeal complex 
is pulled upward and forward by contraction of the suprahyoid 
and thyrohyoid muscles to tuck the larynx under the base of the 
tongue. At the same time, the epiglottis inverts to seal the  
laryngeal vestibule. 60 T ogether, these mechanisms prevent aspi -
ration of foreign materials into the trachea before and during 
swallowing.
The UES opening is necessary for entry of the bolus into the 
esophagus. The UES is closed at rest because of tonic muscle 
contraction 14,28; however, it opens because of three crucial  
factors: (1) relaxation of the cricopharyngeus muscle; (2) con -
traction of the suprahyoid and thyrohyoid muscles, which pull 
the hyolaryngeal complex forward, opening the sphincter; and 
(3) pressure of the descending bolus, which serves to distend 
the UES. 96 These mechanisms are essential for bolus entry into 
the esophagus.
Esophageal Stage
Once the bolus has passed through the UES, it is propelled 
down the esophagus by peristalsis. The two main parts of the 
peristaltic wave are an initial wave of relaxation that accommo -
dates the bolus and a wave of contraction that propels it. In the 
upright position, gravity assists peristalsis. The lower esophageal 
sphincter (LES), held closed by tonic muscle contraction be -
tween swallows, then relaxes during the swallow because of pres -
sure of the descending bolus, allowing passage of the bolus into 
the stomach. 70
Coordination of Swallowing and Breathing
Swallowing and breathing share a common anatomic pathway; 
thus the processes of eating, swallowing, and breathing must be 
tightly coordinated to protect the airway. Muscles of the lips, face, 
tongue, pharynx, larynx, and esophagus are active during breath -
ing and swallowing. Therefore they serve multiple purposes, in -
cluding maintenance of airway patency, airway protection, and 
bolus propulsion. Supporting this idea is the evidence of a tight 
neural link between swallowing and breathing, which has resulted 
from the work of many neurophysiologists who have identified 
and detailed specialized neural networks in the brainstem and 
cortex.21,41,73,75 Further, single neurons within these medullary 
networks have been found to demonstrate multifunctionality in 
the control of both behaviors.41
In healthy adults, swallowing supersedes respiration, 72,79,80 
typically occurring during the expiratory phase of respiration 
between middle and lower lung volumes 72 with an obligate 
pause in respiration to accommodate swallowing. 80 Initiation of 
the swallow under these conditions poses a distinct physiologic 
advantage for protective mechanisms, such as hyolaryngeal an -
terior-superior movement, airway closure, and pharyngoesoph -
ageal segment opening. 9 Onset of the respiratory pause, which 
averages from 1 to 1.5 seconds during liquid swallows, has  
also been associated with airway protection through a momen -
tary medialization of the true vocal folds followed by complete 
vocal fold adduction during anterior-superior hyolaryngeal  
motion.65 Typically, respiration resumes during the descent of 
the larynx at the later stages of the swallow and is marked by a 
brief exhalation. 65
Disruption of the tight neural coupling between the central 
control of breathing and swallowing has been observed in condi -
tions such as aging, neurologic or neuromuscular disease, and 
cancers of the head and neck, resulting in variation in the stabil -
ity of respiratory-swallowing coordination. 6,24,35,67 The coupling 
of respiratory-swallowing phase patterning also changes based on 
the characteristics of the swallowing task. Cued vs. spontaneous 
cup drinking, postural maneuvers, variation in bolus volume, as 
well as modifications of food and liquid texture4,23,69,79 have been 
shown to alter the phase (i.e., inspiratory, expiratory) in which 
the swallow occurs as well as the timing and duration of the  
respiratory pause during the swallow.66 These alterations in respi-
ratory-swallowing phase patterns have been associated with in -
creases in the presence of aspiration and swallowing impairment, 
so clinicians should not only be aware but also identify and opti -
mize peripheral inputs to stabilize aberrant respiratory-swallowing 
patterns.
Neurophysiology of Swallowing
Swallowing is a dynamic, multilayered sensorimotor process  
involving a complex neuronal network. The precise pattern of 
sequential muscle activation and inhibition is dependent on 
portions of the supratentorial (cortical and subcortical), in -
fratentorial (brainstem), and peripheral nervous system (motor 
and sensory). 97 The central nervous system mediates the swal -
lowing process throughout each phase. During the swallow’s 
oral preparatory and oral transport phases, cortical regions such 
as the primary and secondary sensorimotor cortices are espe -
cially active. These phases consist mainly of voluntary behaviors 
(i.e., bolus preparation and mastication). The pharyngeal phase 
involves several cortical and subcortical structures. These in -
clude the primary and secondary sensorimotor cortices, insula, 
anterior and posterior cingulate cortices, basal ganglia, amyg -
dala, hypothalamus, and substantia nigra. 27,100 Inhibition of the 
swallow is important at times, such as the oral preparatory and 
oral transport phases of the swallow, as it would be inefficient 
and ineffective to trigger a pharyngeal swallow during bolus 
preparation.97 Cortical representation of swallowing in healthy 
individuals is bilateral but asymmetric, especially in the motor 
and premotor cortices, 100 thought to be caused by a dominant 
swallowing hemisphere, which has important rehabilitation im -
plications particularly in individuals with conditions such as 
unilateral stroke.
The second level of neurophysiologic control for swallowing 
involves the medulla within the brainstem. This region is active 
during swallowing, most notably during the pharyngeal and 
esophageal phases. The medullary central pattern generator 
(CPG) regulates and coordinates these phases of the swallow

(Fig. 3.2). The CPG is bilaterally represented; evidence of this 
is seen in studies in which unilateral stimulation triggers a  
swallow on the ipsilateral oropharyngeal side. 21,22 Within this 
CPG, two groups of interneurons are integrated: the dorsal 
swallowing group (DSG) and the ventral swallowing group 
(VSG).41 When sensory signals from the oropharyngeal area 
(via cranial nerves [CN] IX and X) reach the nucleus of the 
solitary tract and reticular formation, they excite interneurons 
housed within the DSG, initiating motor signaling to the 
neighboring VSG, which then sequences and coordinates  
the motor input. The signal from the VSG is then passed on to 
the hypoglossal motor nucleus (CN XII) and nucleus ambigu -
ous (CN X), which initiates motor activity in the swallowing 
musculature.
41 These motor and sensory neurons responsible 
for swallowing are located in the same medullary sites as neu -
rons that control respiration, enabling efficient coordination of 
swallowing and breathing.
Muscle movements for swallowing are controlled by neurons 
localized within the brainstem. These include the trigeminal (CN 
V), facial (CN VII), and hypoglossal (CN XII) motor nuclei, 
the nucleus ambiguus (NA) (CN IX and X) and the dorsal motor 
nucleus of the vagus nerve (CN X), as well as cervical spinal neu -
rons (C1 and C3).
22,26,74 However, not all these motoneurons 
participate equally during swallowing, with the primary motor 
nuclei involved in swallowing being the hypoglossal motor  
nucleus (CN XII) and the NA. These nuclei provide innervation 
for the intrinsic and extrinsic muscles of the tongue as well as the 
pharynx, larynx, and upper esophagus, making them crucial for 
swallowing.
Neurons responsible for sensory regulation are also located 
within the brainstem. General sensory information from the oral, 
pharyngeal, and esophageal mucosa is sent to the nucleus of the

solitary tract and the neighboring reticular formation. The 
tongue is the source of a tremendous amount of sensory input; 
therefore multiple cranial nerves are responsible for the relay of 
information such as temperature, texture, size, and volume of 
the bolus. General sensory information for the anterior two-
thirds of the tongue is provided by the trigeminal nerve (CN V), 
while the posterior one-third is innervated by the glossopharyn -
geal nerve (CN IX). Special sensory information, such as taste, 
has been shown to alter swallowing physiology in oral and pha -
ryngeal phases
57,63 with innervation provided by CN VII (facial, 
anterior two-thirds of the tongue) and CN IX (poster one-third 
of the tongue).
8
Because swallowing is a complex, tightly coordinated process 
that involves movements within the oral cavity, pharynx, larynx, 
and esophagus, any impairment within this system (structural or 
neural) can result in swallowing impairment.
Dysphagia
Dysphagia is simply defined as difficulty swallowing. It results 
from various diseases and disorders
7,48 and can lead to significant 
complications such as dehydration, malnutrition, pneumonia, or 
airway obstruction. It is also associated with diminished quality 
of life
98 and mortality. 71 Functional or structural deficits of the 
oral cavity, pharynx, larynx, and esophagus can lead to dyspha-
gia, and swallowing disorders are frequently described in terms of 
stage affected: oral, pharyngeal, or esophageal. Regardless of the 
site, it is helpful to consider whether a given swallowing impair -
ment affects food transport (preparation and propulsion of the 
bolus), airway protection (prevention of laryngeal penetration/
aspiration), or both because of the implications for rehabilitative 
intervention.

55Chapter 3 Rehabilitation of Swallowing Disorders
Oral Phase
The oral preparatory and oral transit stages of the swallow involve 
movements of the lips, tongue, cheeks, and jaw for the purpose of 
mastication, bolus preparation, and bolus transport. Weakness or 
uncoordinated movements of any of these structures can result in 
impairment. For example, a weak lip seal may result in anterior 
spillage of the bolus, especially with liquids. Impairments of the 
tongue and cheeks may lead to reduced bolus formation or bolus 
retention in the lateral sulcus. 70 Premature spillage of the bolus 
from the oral cavity into the throat may arise from impaired 
tongue base function. Additionally, impaired lip control for suck-
ing or insufficient velopharyngeal seal, as well as reduced mastica-
tion, can result from structural abnormalities such as cleft lip/
palate or malalignment of the teeth, respectively.
Pharyngeal Phase
Pharyngeal impairments are difficult to visualize during clinical 
assessment, necessitating instrumental evaluation. Although 
signs and symptoms of pharyngeal disorders can be observed at 
the bedside, such as coughing, choking, and multiple swallows, 
it is impossible to determine the cause of these behaviors without 
instrumental assessment. Abnormalities in the pharyngeal phase 
may include impaired swallow initiation, ineffective bolus pro -
pulsion, pharyngeal retention, and airway invasion of the bolus  
(laryngeal penetration and/or aspiration). Although retention of 
all or part of the bolus in the pharyngeal recesses can occur  
because of impaired tongue base retraction, similar findings can 
be produced by weakness of the pharyngeal constrictor muscula -
ture. Reduced epiglottic inversion is often seen on instrumental 
evaluation, which is a passive movement dependent upon bolus 
pressures, base of tongue retraction, and hyolaryngeal eleva -
tion.70 When the epiglottis does not invert during the swallow, it 
may act as a physical barrier, contributing to retention of the 
bolus in the valleculae after swallowing. Cervical osteophytes are 
a structural abnormality common in older adults, which may 
contribute to dysphagia by narrowing the food pathway and  
directing the bolus toward the airway. 19 Other structural impair -
ments to consider are webs and strictures in the pharynx or 
sphincters.
Airway protective mechanisms are crucial for swallowing 
safety; however, they are not always effective. For dysphagia reha-
bilitation, laryngeal penetration is defined as passage of material 
into the larynx but not through the vocal folds, while aspiration is 
defined as passage of material through the vocal folds.70 Failure of 
protective mechanisms—as demonstrated by reduced laryngeal 
elevation, incomplete laryngeal vestibule closure, or inadequate vocal 
fold closure caused by weakness, paralysis, or anatomic fixation—can 
result in laryngeal penetration or aspiration. 70 Laryngeal penetra-
tion and/or microscopic aspiration can be observed in healthy 
individuals. Still, aspiration visible on instrumental examination 
is pathologic and often associated with an increased risk of aspira-
tion pneumonia or airway obstruction. 64 Typically, a robust  
reflexive cough or throat clear is the response to aspiration, but 
laryngeal sensation is often impaired in those with dysphagia. 30 
Silent aspiration, or aspiration in the absence of observable  
response, has been reported in 25% to 30% of patients referred 
for dysphagia evaluation. 30,54 The consequences of aspiration are 
highly variable and tolerated differently among individuals based 
on factors such as quantity of the aspirate, depth of aspirated 
material in the airway, physical properties of the aspirate, the  
individual’s pulmonary clearance efficiency, general pulmonary  
status, and overall health condition.84
Impaired UES opening is another finding commonly observed 
on instrumental exam, which can cause partial or even total ob -
struction of the foodway, increasing the risk of aspiration after the 
swallow.70 Failure of the UES to open can be structural, caused by 
a web or stricture, or functional, because of muscle weakness. It is 
sometimes difficult to differentiate these conditions, so empiric 
dilation is recommended.
Esophageal Dysphagia
Esophageal dysphagia often results from either a physical obstruc-
tion or motility disorder that prevents food and liquids from eas -
ily passing through the esophagus. Esophageal motor disorders 
include conditions of hyperactivity, hypoactivity, or incoordina -
tion of the esophageal musculature and can often be asymptom -
atic.76 These conditions may contribute to ineffective peristaltic 
movement and retention of swallowed material in the esophagus 
with subsequent regurgitation into the pharynx, leading to an 
increased risk of aspiration. Some causes of esophageal dysphagia 
include achalasia, esophageal spasm, stricture, esophageal tumors, 
foreign bodies, scleroderma, and gastroesophageal reflux disease. 
Diagnosis of esophageal dysphagia requires instrumental assess -
ment, and individuals should be referred to a gastroenterologist 
for further evaluation.
Conditions Resulting in Dysphagia
Dysphagia can occur because of multiple disorders. For that reason, 
careful determination of the underlying etiology of dysphagia 
may necessitate a broad diagnostic approach, including careful 
history, neurologic examination, and relevant studies (e.g., radio-
logic, electromyography, lab, biopsy). One of the most common 
causes of dysphagia is stroke. Dysphagia has been reported in 
65% of stroke patients, only half of whom are symptomatic. 13 
Many recover within the first 2 weeks, but dysphagia can be severe 
and persistent. Brainstem lesions often result in the most severe 
forms of dysphagia, given the proximity to major swallow cen -
ters.41,103 Dysphagia as a result of a stroke may impact all phases 
of the swallow with signs and symptoms such as loss of oral con -
trol, reduced laryngeal elevation, insufficient UES opening, vocal 
fold weakness, dyscoordination of the swallow, impaired bolus 
propulsion, and diminished airway protection. More severe signs 
and symptoms of dysphagia are observed following bilateral cere -
bral lesions, as there is bilateral cortical representation for swallow 
function. By contrast, brainstem motor nuclei innervate only  
ipsilateral muscles, so lesions of cranial nerves or their nuclei can 
result in unilateral sensory or lower motoneuron dysfunction. 31 
Regardless of the type of stroke, pneumonia causes the highest 
attributable mortality of all medical complications following a 
stroke and may affect up to as many as one-third of all stroke 
survivors.2 Most available data suggest that poststroke pneumonia 
is often caused by aspiration.2
Dysphagia is very often one of the first noted symptoms of 
neurologic disease. 58 Oral dysphagia is common in Parkinson 
disease and is characterized by tremor, dyskinesia, and bradykine-
sia in the lips, tongue, jaw, and larynx, which impairs oral and 
pharyngeal swallow function. 1 Symptoms of dysphagia in ad -
vanced disease include drooling, jaw rigidity, impulsive feeding 
behavior, and dysfunction in the UES and LES. In Alzheimer 
disease, agnosia for food within the oral cavity and subsequent

oral holding and incoordination of swallowing are common.  
Dysphagia in motoneuron diseases such as amyotrophic lateral 
sclerosis occurs because of degeneration of motoneurons in the 
brain and spinal cord, resulting in weakness in muscles of mastica-
tion, respiration, and swallowing. Early signs of bulbar involvement 
may include lingual and labial weakness, with bulbar involvement 
associated with a worse prognosis secondary to the high risk of pul-
monary aspiration and malnutrition. Progressive loss of muscle 
function can result in severe dysphagia, so close monitoring of the 
individual’s swallowing function is recommended. Bulbar and facial 
muscles are also affected in approximately one-third of those with 
myasthenia gravis, leading to weakness of mastication, nasal regurgi-
tation, and other symptoms such as poor gag reflex, limited palate 
elevation, and tongue weakness.
Swallowing impairment is common in the case of tumors of 
the oral cavity, pharynx, and larynx because of tissue loss or  
dysfunction. T reatment for these tumors often involves surgical 
excision of structures, chemotherapy, or radiation therapy, all of 
which negatively impact the swallow. The tumors themselves can 
decrease mobility or cause fixation of the tongue, soft palate, 
pharynx, or larynx while also affecting the afferent fibers from the 
mucosa. T ogether, these changes can lead to decreased transport 
of food and liquid boluses and decreased airway protection.
As mentioned, therapies such as radiation therapy can contrib-
ute to dysphagia. Radiation can lead to fibrosis of the oral cavity, 
pharynx, and larynx, with effects sometimes evident immediately 
but also much later in the course of treatment. In the acute stages, 
there may be changes in the mucosa (e.g., erythema, mucositis, 
ulceration), taste buds can have decreased or altered acuity, and 
there is reduced production or thickened saliva. Late effects  
include fibrosis resulting in decreased pharyngeal motility and 
leading to delayed swallow initiation, diminished pharyngeal 
transport, and ineffective laryngeal protection. 47 Changes to the 
salivary glands may also become permanent, leading to xerosto -
mia, which can contribute to reduced bolus formation and im -
paired timing of oral and pharyngeal transport.
Postoperative dysphagia is prevalent across a variety of proce -
dures. Dysphagia occurs in approximately one-half of individuals 
following anterior cervical fusion, 99 and patients with multiple 
surgical levels have a higher risk of dysphagia compared with 
those who only undergo fusion at one level.16 Decreased laryngeal 
sensation, pooling of secretions in the pharynx and larynx, im -
paired swallow initiation, and aspiration are common following 
endotracheal intubation and ventilator dependency. An inflated 
cuff does not eliminate aspiration. 32 A tracheostomy tube can al -
ter normal pharyngeal aerodynamics, eliminating the positive 
subglottic pressure normally associated with swallowing, which 
then hampers laryngeal protective reflexes. T racheostomy occlu -
sion through tracheostomy speaking valves, caps, or healing 
grants voluntary pharyngoesophageal clearance by allowing the 
buildup of subglottic pressure, which is necessary for an effective 
cough to help clear any penetrated materials. 56
It is also essential to be aware of the impact of medication regi-
mens on swallow function. Many medications, including those 
available over the counter, can impact swallowing. 89 Medications 
may impair consciousness, reduce muscular coordination and sen-
sory functions, or impact saliva production. Other medications 
bring on conditions such as mouth ulcerations, glossitis, esophagi-
tis, and stomatitis. Some classes of drugs that are particularly  
concerning for negative impact on swallow function include 
nonsteroidal antiinflammatories, antibiotics, antihistamines, anti-
spasmodics, anticholinergics, antimuscarinics, antihypertensives, 
anxiolytics, antipsychotics, anti-Parkinson disease agents, anticon-
vulsants, and even vitamins. In addition to medications that affect 
motor and sensory functions or damage mucosa, any medication 
that causes sedation can increase the risk of aspiration and impair 
airway clearance (Table 3.1).
Evaluation of Dysphagia
T o effectively address swallowing disorders and reduce the risk 
for dysphagia-associated medical complications, the health care 
team must first complete a careful swallowing assessment using 
appropriate diagnostic procedures. The purpose of the swallowing 
evaluation is to assess dysphagia and, when appropriate, make 
recommendations for diet, swallow safety strategies, and rehabili-
tation interventions. A complete swallowing workup typically 
involves a bedside swallow screening followed by a clinical bedside

identified during the clinical exam. The instrumental exam aims to 
determine the presence or absence of airway invasion objectively, 
clarify the relationship between symptoms and pathophysiology, 
and guide decision making regarding therapeutic intervention.
The two most common instrumental swallowing assessments 
across medical settings are the videofluoroscopic swallow study 
(VFSS) and the fiberoptic endoscopic evaluation of the swallow 
(FEES). In recent years, additional instrumental assessment tools, 
such as high-resolution pharyngeal manometry (HRPM) and 
computed tomography swallowing studies (CTSS), have emerged, 
giving clinicians more options for assessing swallowing function. 
Regardless of the type of assessment used to evaluate swallow 
function, the examiner should recognize that the results represent 
only a moment in time; therefore they need to be interpreted in 
the context of the patient’s clinical presentation. Many variables, 
such as the underlying medical condition, medication regimen, 
cognitive status, age, feeding status, functional status, as well as 
unknown factors, can potentially influence function positively or 
negatively.59 Moreover, it is not uncommon for individuals with 
dysphagia to demonstrate variable swallowing performance de -
pending on the time of day because of fatigue or the timing of 
medications. The variability of swallowing performance through-
out the day may be especially prominent for aging individuals and 
those with progressive neurologic diseases. Therefore it is impera-
tive to interpret the results of the swallowing assessment in con -
junction with the overall clinical picture.
Bedside/Clinical Swallow Assessments
Swallow Screenings
The swallow screening is usually the first step in identifying an 
increased risk of dysphagia. Swallow screenings may take on a 
variety of forms with a range of protocols, all with the purpose 
to accurately identify individuals who present a risk of dyspha -
gia and expedite referral to a speech pathologist for further 
evaluation.15 Generally, the screening should be quick and 
minimally invasive, with the ability to be used by any team 
member. The swallow screening may identify risk factors for 
dysphagia using self-report, clinical history, and/or direct clini -
cal observation.
Most swallow screening protocols include a brief history,  
examination, and swallowing challenge, typically swallowing wa -
ter. The 3-ounce water swallowing test is common. Individuals are 
asked to consume 3 ounces of water uninterrupted without any 
overt signs of aspiration (i.e., cough, choking, throat clearing, wet 
voice quality). If the person cannot drink the entire amount un -
interrupted or demonstrates signs of aspiration immediately after 
consuming the water, this is considered a “fail” on the screening, 
and the individual is considered at risk for aspiration. One com -
monly used tool that includes a brief cognitive screen and an oral 
examination in addition to the swallowing challenge is the Yale 
Swallow Protocol.55
Other swallow screenings reported in the literature also use 
water with volumes ranging from 5 to 90 mL and different crite -
ria for pass/fail.17,68,105,106 The implementation of swallow screen-
ing tools has resulted in a reduction of pneumonia rates, 20,34 and 
screening tools that include swallow challenges yield the best  
patient outcomes. 34,101 Furthermore, using water as part of a 
screening makes the screening relatively easy to administer.
Clinical Swallowing Evaluation
The clinical swallowing evaluation (CSE) is often conducted 
when signs and symptoms of dysphagia are identified by screen -
ing, patient report, or observation by a direct caregiver or family 
member. Although the CSE does not allow for any direct obser -
vation of the physiology of the pharyngeal phase of the swallow, 
other pertinent information can be obtained related to swallow 
function. The CSE has five components: (1) medical history  
review and patient/family interview, (2) cognitive/mental status 
function, (3) oral motor and sensory function, (4) laryngeal and 
pulmonary function, and (5) administration of oral swallowing 
trials.59 Review of the patient’s medical history and current 
medical status includes the medical course of treatment, admit -
ting diagnosis, lab values, nutrition status, medications, and  
respiratory status. The clinician considers the impact of any sur -
gical procedures and the results of any prior dysphagia assess -
ments or treatments.
The cognitive-linguistic evaluation elicits information regard -
ing the patient’s level of alertness, orientation, memory (i.e., long-
term, immediate, delayed), attention, reasoning, problem solving, 
and the individual’s basic speech and language function. In addi-
tion to communication of basic needs, these skills also impact the 
patient’s ability to follow directions and implement strategies for 
safe swallowing. For example, significantly increased impulsivity 
and distractibility as seen in right middle cerebral artery strokes 
can impact safety and subsequently raise the risk of aspiration 
considerably. Therefore all these elements must be factored into 
the speech-language pathologist’s (SLP’s) decision making and 
treatment planning as they relate to patient safety.
The primary focus of the oral exam is the evaluation of the jaw, 
lips, tongue, dentition, and hard/soft palate structures. These 
structures are assessed for any abnormalities with strength, sym -
metry at rest and upon movement, tone, atypical movement (e.g., 
apraxia), and structural deviations related to surgical/acquired 
condition or congenital factors. Preliminary studies have emerged 
demonstrating potential objective measures of oral strength and 
range that may be identified during the initial evaluation as pre -
dictors of swallowing outcomes. 51,55 Dentition and condition of 
the oral mucosa are also evaluated during the oral motor assess -
ment. If xerostomia is noted on the oral exam, rigorous oral care 
is indicated because colonization of the oral cavity with bacteria is 
exacerbated in people with poor salivary flow.
During the oral sensory component of the assessment, the 
clinician evaluates the individual’s response to touch and tem -
perature bilaterally for the chin, lips, tongue, inner cheeks, and 
palate. Attempts to elicit a gag reflex may reveal a sudden tonic 
reaction to a noxious stimulus in the posterior oropharynx. The 
absence of a gag reflex is not a clinical predictor of dysphagia, nor 
does it protect against aspiration because a subset of the normal 
population does not present with a gag reflex. 52 In addition, pul-
monary status is assessed through observation of the individual’s 
respiratory rate at rest along with an assessment of volitional and 
reflexive (if elicited) throat clearing and cough production. Laryn-
geal function is indirectly assessed by evaluating the strength of 
the cough and vocal quality, vocal loudness, and pitch range. 
Nasal resonance is also assessed to indirectly determine the integ -
rity of the velopharyngeal port.
The results of these initial components of the CSE will guide 
the clinician’s decision to attempt or refrain from administering 
food and liquid trials. When attempting swallow trials, clinicians 
often start by asking the individual to produce a saliva swallow,

Instrumental Swallow Assessments
Videofluoroscopic Swallow Study
VFSS is a standard examination for the diagnosis and treatment 
of dysphagia, also known as modified barium swallow. It allows 
for a comprehensive oral, pharyngeal, and esophageal function 
evaluation. The procedure involves swallowing a contrast agent 
or food containing a contrast agent, while using real-time x-ray 
fluoroscopy to observe the movement of the swallowing struc -
tures and the flow of the contrast agent. This examination allows 
for the identification of both structural and functional impair -
ments and assists in the determination of safe and appropriate 
interventions, such as whether oral intake is feasible or if alterna -
tive means of nutrition are necessary. It also involves decision 
making regarding compensatory strategies such as positioning, 
dietary modifications, and alternative swallowing methods for 
rehabilitation interventions.
Specifically, VFSS provides insights into oral motor function, 
bolus formation and propulsion, initiation of pharyngeal swallow 
timing, velopharyngeal closure, laryngeal closure, and UES open-
ing. During this assessment, the clinician observes oral control 
and assesses pharyngeal clearance as well as airway protection, 
including laryngeal penetration and aspiration occurrences before, 
during, and after the swallow. VFSS is essential for detecting silent 
aspiration missed during CSEs and for guiding treatment deci -
sions. The procedure typically occurs in radiology departments, 
involving collaboration between radiologists or physiatrists and 
SLPs. Clinicians trained in VFSS administration and interpreta -
tion ensure radiation safety during the procedure. VFSS protocols 
vary based on patient presentation, involving assessments with 
different bolus consistencies (i.e., thin, slightly thick, mildly 
thick, moderately thick, extremely thick liquids; regular, soft and 
bitesize, minced and moist, pureed, liquidized solids), sizes (e.g., 
1, 5, 10, or 20 mL, and uncontrolled size), and presentation 
methods (e.g., syringe, cup, or spoon; self-intake or assisted), of -
ten introducing compensatory strategies to optimize swallowing 
safety and efficiency. The procedure aims to minimize radiation 
exposure while providing comprehensive insights into swallowing 
function. In the case of high-risk patients, careful consideration 
should be given to determine the maximum competence of the 
individual’s swallowing function. Challenging the system and 
knowing the patient’s maximum capability are helpful in under -
standing the underlying pathophysiology of the swallowing im -
pairment and appropriate treatment directions.
The VFSS can also evaluate esophageal function. Although 
standardized evaluation methods have not yet been established, 
it is recommended that evaluation of esophageal function be 
performed in conjunction with oropharyngeal swallow function 
as much as possible. The contrast medium is ingested in the 
frontal view, and organic and functional abnormalities are ob -
served from the flow of the contrast medium. The presence or 
absence of residuals, the location of residuals, and the state of 
esophageal peristalsis are observed. In rare cases, a tracheoesoph -
ageal fistula may be detected, so alternate fields of view may also 
be useful (Fig. 3.3).
Fiberoptic Endoscopic Evaluation of Swallowing
FEES is a common instrumental assessment that allows for direct 
visualization of the pharynx and larynx, accumulated oropharyn -
geal secretion levels, swallowing function, and sensory ability in 
persons of all ages from infants to older adults. The flexible fiber-
optic laryngoscope is equipped with a chip camera attached to a 
light source and video recorder, and the examiner (i.e., endosco -
pist) visualizes the image either directly through an eyepiece or on 
a monitor when using the camera attached to the laryngoscope. 
Images can be recorded for further analysis and playback for 
therapeutic/educational purposes.
T o perform the procedure, the laryngoscope is passed through 
the nares following application of a topical anesthetic. Because of 
the placement of the laryngoscope, the oral phase and the esoph -
ageal phase of the swallow cannot be directly visualized during the 
FEES assessment. Additionally, there is a white-out period at  
the height of the swallow during which the pharyngeal squeeze at 
the level of the camera on the distal end of the scope causes light 
to reflect into the endoscope and obscure the view of the bolus 
and pharyngeal structures. For this reason, the examiner must 
make inferences based on observed pre- and postswallow compo -
nents of the FEES.49
The typical FEES protocol includes an assessment of the ana -
tomic structures of the larynx at rest and in movement, the ac -
cumulated oropharyngeal secretion level, and bolus flow of vari -
ous foods and liquids while swallowing. The examiner will 
instruct the individual to produce various speech and vocal tasks 
as well as a dry swallow before presenting foods and liquids that 
may be treated with food dye to enhance visualization of bolus 
flow during swallowing. If difficulty swallowing is observed, 
compensatory strategies (e.g., postural changes, swallowing ma -
neuvers, and bolus modifications) may be introduced to identify 
optimal conditions for swallowing performance. Additional as -
sessment of laryngopharyngeal sensation and airway protection is 
possible when performing the more extensive flexible endoscopic 
evaluation of swallowing with sensory testing. Sensory discrimi -
nation thresholds are determined by presenting pressure and 
duration-controlled air pulses from a port on the laryngoscope to 
the mucosa to elicit the laryngeal adductor reflex, thereby allow -
ing objective sensory discrimination testing as well as testing for 
individuals with cognitive deficits who are unable to reliably  
respond to psychophysical testing. 3
In addition to the ability to identify pooled oropharyngeal 
secretions, the amount of postswallow pharyngeal residue, and 
laryngeal penetration and/or aspiration, FEES is beneficial in 
other ways. For example, reduced vocal fold mobility has been 
associated with increased risk of aspiration, 53 and FEES allows 
for direct observation of vocal fold anatomy and movement. 
FEES may also be used as biofeedback in appropriate individuals

High-Resolution Pharyngeal Manometry
HRPM is a diagnostic test that provides a quantitative assessment 
of intraluminal swallowing-related pressure events. Introduced 
just over a decade ago
102 as a means to evaluate swallow function, 
it involves the placement of a catheter with at least 36 solid-state 
circumferential pressure sensors, which are spaced at approxi-
mately 1-cm intervals to measure pressure events and bolus flow 
during the pharyngeal and esophageal phases of the swallow.
78 
The pressure forces measured by a HRPM catheter are influenced 
by several factors, including the strength and sequencing of neural 
activation of swallowing musculature, luminal diameter, pharyn -
geal wall thickness, and the presence and compressibility of lumi-
nal content.
Individuals with dysphagia often present with weak but ap-
propriately sequenced pharyngeal pressures, which indicates  
reduced force generation capacity of the pharyngeal musculature. 
In such individuals, HRPM can objectively diagnose pharyngeal

With the HRPM catheter in place, individuals with dysphagia are

Treatment of Dysphagia
The goal of dysphagia treatment is to restore swallowing skills 
while continuously maintaining adequate nutrition, hydration, 
and safe (aspiration-free) swallowing for the individual. When 
planning treatment, the clinician must consider the individual’s 
disease process and potential for recovery of function along with 
other important factors, such as motivation, ability to participate 
in a rehabilitation program, response to interventions, and avail -
ability of caregiver support. Early treatment of dysphagia has been 
shown to reduce the risk for aspiration pneumonia, reduce medi-
cal complications related to malnutrition and dehydration, and 
reduce the length of the hospital stay.
45 Equally important is the 
amelioration of barriers that decrease an individual’s ability to 
participate in and enjoy the pleasures of eating orally. Small 
physiologic improvements in swallowing that do not result in an 
increase in quality of life miss the true goal of rehabilitation.

Dysphagia treatment approaches to improve the safety and ef-
ficiency of the swallow can be described in terms of compensatory 
or restorative strategies. The goal of compensatory swallowing 
strategies is not to change swallowing physiology but to prevent 
symptoms of dysphagia to maintain safety and ensure adequate 
nutrition and hydration. These techniques do not result in long-
term physiologic changes and include strategies such as volume 
and texture modifications of foods and liquids as well as postural 
changes and maneuvers. Alternatively, restorative treatment in -
volves swallowing exercises used to treat dysphagia by altering 
swallowing physiology and promoting long-term changes. Restor-
ative exercises are expected to impact swallowing mechanics and 
bolus flow. Depending on the physiologic impairment identified 
by the instrumental swallowing exam, a combination of compen-
satory and restorative rehabilitation strategies may be implemented 
to both manage dysphagia symptoms and improve swallowing 
physiology.
107
Compensatory Strategies in Swallowing 
Rehabilitation
Compensatory swallowing strategies are recommended when the 
objective of treatment is to prevent symptoms of dysphagia in or-
der to maintain safety and ensure adequate nutrition, not change 
swallowing physiology. With a focus on improving quality of life, 
the goal is for the individual to regain the ability to participate in 
social activities and to enjoy the pleasures of eating and drinking 
through volume and texture modifications, sensory enhancement, 
and implementation of postural swallowing strategies.
Following a thorough assessment of swallow function, which 
typically includes an instrumental exam, the clinician must first 
determine if it is safe to feed the individual by mouth and in what 
capacity (i.e., begin with therapeutic oral trials vs. small or full 
meals). Decisions about recommendations for both diet modifica-
tions and swallowing maneuvers should be based on identification 
of impaired physiology observed during the instrumental exam.
Postural techniques alter the direction of bolus flow using 
gravity or changes in anatomic positioning during a swallow. 
These strategies should be tested and proven effective during in -
strumental evaluation before recommending their use. Examples 
include collapsing the pharyngeal space by turning the head to -
ward the impaired side as well as performing a chin tuck to widen 
the valleculae and positioning the epiglottis in a more protective 
posterior position to reduce laryngeal penetration or aspiration.
Sensory enhancement techniques are also part of the treatment 
strategies. These interventions aim to increase the sensory input to 
the brainstem, thereby maximizing the swallow response. For ex -
ample, taste and chemesthesis can increase oral and pharyngeal 
transit in individuals with dysphagia.
88 A sour bolus has been 
found to elicit earlier onset of lingual activity to propel a bolus to 
the oropharynx and trigger a pharyngeal motor response in indi -
viduals with stroke or neurologic disorder.
61 The application of 
thermal tactile stimulation or thermal gustatory stimulation is 
somewhat controversial, but some data suggest that it may in -
crease corticobulbar activity, which has been associated with re -
covery of swallow function following stroke.
90 The clinician uses 
a chilled laryngeal mirror or iced lemon glycerin swab to stroke 
the anterior faucial arches in the posterior oral cavity to increase 
sensory input, thereby facilitating a timely swallow response. 
These strategies have been shown to result in immediate changes 
in swallowing function, but long-lasting effects have not been 
proven (Fig. 3.7).
Modification of food and liquid properties (texture and vis -
cosity) as well as presentation characteristics (volume and tem -
perature) are common therapeutic approaches, although most 
clinicians agree that this should be the treatment of last resort.  
It is important to standardize terminology and definitions to 
describe texture-modified foods and liquids and to maximize the 
reliability of diet modifications across settings, practitioners, and 
cultures. T o this end, the International Dysphagia Diet Standard-
ization Initiative (IDDSI)
11 was formed with the aim to improve 
the lives of people with dysphagia by standardizing the classifica -
tion and description of food and liquid textures. The IDDSI 
framework consists of a continuum of eight levels (0 through 7), 
where drinks are measured from levels 0 through 4, and foods are 
measured from levels 3 through 7. Liquids range from thin (level 
0) to extremely thick (level 4), while foods range from regular/
easy to chew (level 7) to liquidized (level 3). In addition, the  
IDDSI framework offers a standard approach to caregiver train -
ing by means of a flow test to ensure that thickened liquids are 
mixed to the appropriate viscosity, eliminating the confusion 
posed by the variability of methods previous utilized to thicken 
liquids.
11 IDDSI diet levels have been adopted by many medical 
settings across the world.
For individuals with decreased oral motor strength and/or co -
ordination resulting in difficulty with bolus formation and/or 
bolus transit through the oral cavity, a diet texture change to soft/
bitesize, minced and moist, or pureed may increase the efficiency 
and/or safety of swallowing. Many individuals with neurogenic 
dysphagia are at greatest risk of aspiration with thin liquids be-
cause of poor bolus control, reduced lingual propulsion, delayed 
pharyngeal swallow, or a combination of these impairments. In 
cases such as this, thickened liquids (slightly thick, mildly thick, 
moderately thick, or extremely thick) may be recommended to 
minimize the risk of aspiration.

63Chapter 3 Rehabilitation of Swallowing Disorders
Restorative Strategies: Exercise Training and 
Plasticity Considerations
The oropharyngeal system exhibits considerable plasticity in the 
adaptation of new tasks or sensory experiences in health and 
disease. Therefore the goal of rehabilitative exercises for individu-
als with dysphagia is to change and improve the physiology of 
swallowing in force, speed, and/or timing to promote long-term 
treatment effects. In this way, retraining of the neuromuscular 
system results in neuroplasticity. 10 For example, the proportion 
of neurons in the primary somatosensory cortex (S1) and pri -
mary motor cortex (M1) that are correlated with tongue protru -
sion increases following daily tongue training. Additional studies 
have shown the importance of task-related specificity among 
tongue motor representations and the cortical masticatory area. 93 
The specificity in orofacial gestures and swallowing for targeted 
neuroplasticity interventions suggests that consideration be given 
for stimulus salience (nonswallow task training compared with 
swallow task training) to affect beneficial behavioral changes and 
adaptive plasticity for swallowing and oral motor behaviors. 77
A variety of exercises exist ranging from direct to indirect and 
those that incorporate swallowing or nonswallowing exercises to 
effect change. Many of the swallowing exercises follow the princi-
ples of neuromuscular rehabilitation, including use it or lose it, 
training specificity, repetition, muscle overload, and training inten-
sity to facilitate increased muscle strength. 50,91 For example, the 
strength training principles of overloading and specificity have been 
successfully applied to swallow training. Following the principle of 
specificity, it is best to select exercises that are similar to the target 
behavior.30 The Mendelson and tongue-hold (i.e., Masako) exercises 
apply this principle as the individual is attempting swallowing func-
tion during these exercises. Overloading involves completing exer-
cises at resistance levels higher than typically used. During the 
Mendelson maneuver, the swallow is prolonged, lasting longer than 
the normal duration. When completing the tongue-hold maneuver, 
the anterior tongue is anchored, resulting in increased pharyngeal 
constrictor activity. Examples of exercise training that incorporates 
increased intensity of resistance are the isometric lingual exercise, 
expiratory muscle strength training (EMST; a technique targeting 
the respiratory musculature), effortful swallow, Mendelson maneu-
ver, tongue-hold maneuver, and Shaker exercises. The importance 
of repetition of these exercises to induce change in swallow function 
must also be emphasized. Extensive and prolonged practice im -
proves neural substrates and serves to assist in both the acquisition 
and maintenance of improved swallow function.
Although the body of evidence to support the use of specific 
exercise training has been growing tremendously in recent years, 
not all exercises have sufficient evidence acquired through well-  
designed large studies to support their use. Some interventions have 
proven effective in healthy normal individuals or may have been 
effective in a small number of study participants and therefore may 
warrant further investigation to assist in guiding clinicians to con-
sistently use evidence-based practice. Additionally, individuals with 
cognitive dysfunction may require ongoing 1:1 or close supervision 
and cueing to perform the exercises and multistep maneuvers ef -
fectively. Use of visual biofeedback by endoscopy, EMG, FEES, or 
other means may serve to positively impact accurate performance of 
therapeutic exercises, such as the supraglottic swallow technique, 
and increase effectiveness of the exercise program.
Although nonswallowing exercises do not meet all the princi -
ples of neuroplasticity (i.e., specificity and overload), some have 
been shown to have good evidence for their efficacy. For example, 
the Shaker head lift exercises use a combination of isometric and 
isokinetic exercises to improve the strength of the suprahyoid 
muscles over time and increase the opening of the UES. Consistent 
performance of this exercise has resulted in favorable long-term 
effects in individuals with dysphagia. EMST has been shown to 
improve both ventilatory and nonventilatory functions and has 
reported positive effects on swallowing.50
Other nonswallowing exercises include the Lee Silverman 
Voice T reatment (LSVT), chin tuck against resistance (CTAR), 
and lingual strengthening exercises. LSVT is a proven program 
used to improve vocal intensity in persons with Parkinson disease; 
however, it has been found in pilot studies to produce gains in 
swallowing and cough function caused by a shared neurologic 
network.95 CTAR is an exercise that targets the suprahyoid mus -
cles to improve function of the muscles that comprise the UES. A 
pilot study compared the use of CTAR exercise in the treatment 
group of subacute stroke patients with a traditional therapy regi -
men received by the control group; it revealed that CTAR treat -
ment resulted in significantly improved laryngeal elevation and 
epiglottic closure as well as pharyngeal clearance and greater air -
way protection in the experimental group. 87 Finally, lingual exer-
cises have been investigated using the Iowa Oral Performance 
Instrument, a device that measures the maximal amount of 
tongue pressure a person can produce by lingual compression of a 
tongue bulb against the hard palate. Lingual exercises have been 
shown to have short-term effects in healthy normal participants 
but mixed results for swallowing safety and efficiency. 92 Further 
study is warranted for all nonswallowing exercises to determine 
whether there is long-term benefit across dysphagia populations.
The use of neuromuscular electrical stimulation (NMES) to 
improve muscle function is not new in rehabilitative medicine but 
has grown in use as a dysphagia treatment method over the past 2 
decades. It has been hypothesized that the use of surface electrodes 
taped to the skin overlying the submental or anterior cervical strap 
muscles (59 seconds on, 1 second off, for 60 continuous seconds) 
will result in strengthened swallowing musculature and a height -
ened sensory feedback loop for swallowing pathways. 29 However, 
contradictory findings have been reported with one study of indi-
viduals with chronic pharyngeal dysphagia showing that electrical 
stimulation actually depressed the hyolaryngeal complex in those 
with little to no hyolaryngeal movement, placing individuals  
at increased risk of airway invasion. 62 Additional research with a 
focus on dosage, timing, and applications in specific patient popu-
lations is needed to determine whether NMES coupled with swal-
lowing therapy may result in beneficial effects (Table 3.3).
Other Interventions for Dysphagia
Surgery is seldom indicated for the treatment of oropharyngeal 
dysphagia. In the case of UES or LES stenosis, dilation can be in-
dicated. Surgery is also performed for Zenker diverticulum when 
the diverticulum is large enough to interfere significantly with 
eating and drinking.109 Cricopharyngeal myotomy is a procedure 
that disrupts the cricopharyngeus muscle for the purpose of reduc-
ing UES pressure. This decreases the resistance to flow from the 
pharynx to the esophagus. However, evidence of its effectiveness is 
limited.14 Botulinum toxin injections are occasionally used to treat 
dysphagia, especially in cases of oromandibular or lingual dystonia, 
trismus, or cricopharyngeal dysfunction with failure of UES relax-
ation.110 In cases of severe dysphagia, permanent tracheostomies in 
combination with laryngectomy or another procedure to close the 
larynx can serve to separate the airway from the pathway of food

Bypassing the Oropharyngeal Swallow Process
When adequate and safe oral intake is not possible, pharyngeal by-
pass procedures eliminate the need for swallowing and provide alter-
native means for achieving nutrition, hydration, and medication 
administration.
46 In addition to dysphagia with NPO status, some 
individuals with dysphagia experience extended mealtimes because 
of a variety of factors, such as physical changes, changes in taste 
perception, anxiety related to oral intake, and cognitive impairment. 
Extended mealtimes (e.g., food intake periods lasting more than 
30 minutes) often result in reduced caloric intake, which can nega-
tively impact nutritional status. In these cases, supplemental means 
of nutritional intake are indicated. Short-term feeding options in-
clude nasogastric and orogastric tubes, which are typically used 
when alternative nutrition will be required for 6 weeks or less. Gas-
trostomy and jejunostomy tubes are often used in cases of severe 
dysphagia when alternative feeding methods will be required long 
term. Although the use of feeding tubes is generally safe, they do not 
necessarily prevent aspiration, and tube feeding may in fact promote 
gastroesophageal reflux with secondary aspiration of stomach con-
tents. In one study, pneumonia developed in up to 44% of patients 
with acute stroke who were fed with gastrostomy. Furthermore, 66% 
of those patients continued to aspirate chronically, with mortality 
rates greater than 50% after 1 year.
18 Measures to minimize aspira-
tion include head elevation, good hygiene with bag and line han-
dling, use of slow and continuous feeding rather than bolus, and 
monitoring for gastric residue. Parenteral nutrition, or feeding intra-
venously, is often given temporarily to those who need an immediate

Summary
In summary, swallowing is a complex sequence of voluntary and re-
flexive movements comprised of multiple phases mediated by cortical 
and subcortical regions of the brain. Impaired swallowing function 
can arise from a wide variety of functional and structural deficits re-
sulting in sensory and/or motor impairment. Consequences of these 
impairments can lead to serious complications including dehydra-
tion, malnutrition, pneumonia, or airway obstruction which is why 
it is important to approach therapeutic intervention with a patient-
centered, multi-disciplinary plan of care. This most often includes 
instrumental assessment to accurately identify biomechanical compo-
nents of the individual’s swallow function to target for compensatory 
or restorative interventions with the goal of maintaining safety while 
maximizing function and quality of life.
